reasoning:
The 6-month T-bill yield (DTB6) plunged to 3.99% on August 1, 2025, driven by an extreme market reaction to a weak July jobs report (just 73k jobs added) and the resignation of a Fed governor. A single-day 14 bps drop from 4.13% typically overshoots underlying economic fundamentals. The FRED statistical tool provides a strong baseline for the short-term mean-reversion, estimating a 71% structural probability of the yield exceeding 3.99% by August 10. Consequently, my first probability is anchored at 0.71.

Over the medium to long term (September 2025 to January 2026), macro conditions point toward sustained Fed easing. Current futures pricing implies markets naturally expect 50–75 basis points in rate cuts over this horizon, meaning the natural trajectory for short-term yields is downward, weakening the probability that the rate will stubbornly remain above 3.99%. 

As dictated by the system's structural constraints, forecast uncertainty must increase symmetrically over time, requiring probabilities for distant resolution dates to progressively converge toward 0.5. To model both the declining economic likelihood of elevated yields and the mandated increase in horizon uncertainty, I monotonically dampen the 0.71 anchor toward a near-coin-flip 0.53 for the final Jan 2026 horizon.
